message.content: Adversaries may develop self-signed SSL/TLS digital certificates to facilitate their operations, leveraging the inherent trust these certificates are designed to instill. Unlike certificates signed by a trusted third-party Certificate Authority (CA), self-signed certificates lack external validation, making them less trustworthy. However, adversaries can use these certificates to encrypt Command and Control (C2) traffic, enable Adversary-in-the-Middle (AitM) attacks, or authenticate malware communications. Notable groups like APT29, Transparent Tribe, and PROMETHIUM have utilized self-signed certificates for these purposes. The potential impact includes compromised communication channels and enhanced evasion of detection mechanisms. Mitigation strategies involve rigorous certificate validation processes, monitoring for unusual certificate issuance, and employing network security measures to detect and block suspicious SSL/TLS traffic.
